root_text.sn22.123:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā ca sāriputto āyasmā ca mahākoṭṭhiko bārāṇasiyaṁ viharanti isipatane migadāye.
root_text.sn22.123:1.2: Atha kho āyasmā mahākoṭṭhiko sāyanhasamayaṁ paṭisallānā vuṭṭhito yenāyasmā sāriputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā …pe… etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.123:2.1: “Sutavatāvuso sāriputta, bhikkhunā katame dhammā yoniso manasi kātabbā”ti?
root_text.sn22.123:2.2: “Sutavatāvuso koṭṭhika, bhikkhunā pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.123:2.5: Sutavatāvuso koṭṭhika, bhikkhunā ime pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.123:2.6: Ṭhānaṁ kho panetaṁ, āvuso, vijjati—yaṁ sutavā bhikkhu ime pañcupādānakkhandhe aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi karonto sotāpattiphalaṁ sacchikareyyā”ti.
root_text.sn22.123:3.1: “Sotāpannena panāvuso sāriputta, bhikkhunā katame dhammā yoniso manasi kātabbā”ti?
root_text.sn22.123:3.2: “Sotāpannenapi kho āvuso koṭṭhika, bhikkhunā ime pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.123:3.3: Ṭhānaṁ kho panetaṁ, āvuso, vijjati—yaṁ sotāpanno bhikkhu ime pañcupādānakkhandhe aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi karonto sakadāgāmiphalaṁ …pe…
root_text.sn22.123:4.1: “Arahatā panāvuso sāriputta, katame dhammā yoniso manasi kātabbā”ti?
root_text.sn22.123:4.2: “Arahatāpi khvāvuso koṭṭhika, ime pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.123:4.3: Natthi, khvāvuso, arahato uttari karaṇīyaṁ, katassa vā paticayo;
root_text.sn22.123:4.4: api ca kho ime dhammā bhāvitā bahulīkatā diṭṭhadhammasukhavihārāya ceva saṁvattanti satisampajaññāya cā”ti.

translation_text.sn22.123:1.1: At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana.
translation_text.sn22.123:1.2: Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, bowed, sat down to one side, and said:
translation_text.sn22.123:2.1: “Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a learned mendicant rationally apply the mind to?”
translation_text.sn22.123:2.2: “A learned mendicant should rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.123:2.4: That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.123:2.5: A learned mendicant should rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.123:2.6: It’s possible that a learned mendicant who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of stream-entry.”
translation_text.sn22.123:3.1: “But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a mendicant stream-enterer rationally apply the mind to?”
translation_text.sn22.123:3.2: “A mendicant stream-enterer should also rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.123:3.3: It’s possible that a mendicant stream-enterer who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of once-return.” …
translation_text.sn22.123:3.4: “It’s possible that a mendicant once-returner who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of non-return.” …
translation_text.sn22.123:3.5: “It’s possible that a mendicant non-returner who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of perfection.”
translation_text.sn22.123:4.1: “But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a perfected one rationally apply the mind to?”
translation_text.sn22.123:4.2: “Reverend Koṭṭhita, a perfected one should rationally apply the mind to the five grasping aggregates as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.123:4.3: A perfected one has nothing more to do, and nothing that needs improvement.
translation_text.sn22.123:4.4: Still, these things, when developed and cultivated, lead to blissful meditation in this life, and also to mindfulness and situational awareness.”
